source_label: Aaron van Wirdum — Taproot, the next major Bitcoin upgrade (2021)
excerpt: If everyone defaulted to spending via the key path when possible, observers could no longer differentiate single-signature transactions from multisignature transactions or smart-contract transactions. The privacy gain depends on adoption: the bigger the anonymity set of plain key-path spends, the more cover any one user gets.
body:
Source excerpt:
If everyone defaulted to spending via the key path when possible, observers could no longer differentiate single-signature transactions from multisignature transactions or smart-contract transactions. The privacy gain depends on adoption: the bigger the anonymity set of plain key-path spends, the more cover any one user gets.

---

Cited by leaf "Taproot's privacy implications":
Before taproot, a 2-of-3 multisig spend, a hashlock contract, and a regular single-sig spend each looked different on chain. After taproot (BIPs 340–342, activated 2021), they can all look like the same thing: one Schnorr signature against one 32-byte key [1]. The trick is that a taproot output's key is a real signing key tweaked by the merkle root of an optional script tree. If the parties cooperate they spend via the key path — one signature, no script revealed — and an observer can't tell whether there was ever a script tree at all.

This matters for fungibility. When most spends look identical, surveillance firms lose one of their cheapest signals: they can no longer cluster wallets by their script style, or charge a risk premium on coins that came out of a multisig setup. It also matters for Lightning channel closes — a cooperative close from a taproot channel is indistinguishable from a single-sig spend [2].

The limits are worth being honest about. Privacy is only equal to the most common output type: as long as plain p2tr key-path spends remain a small minority of the chain, a multisig wallet that always uses the key path still stands out by being a taproot user at all. Script-path spends do reveal one branch of the tree (the rest stays hidden), so a contract that is forced to its non-cooperative path leaks structure. And taproot doesn't help with the address-reuse, common-input-ownership, or amount-correlation heuristics at all — those need coinjoin, payjoin, or silent payments [3].
